author: garyrong
content:
I guess we can generalize the dicussion to reservoir-based refund vs source-based refund. The same situation applies to all revertable state charging (account-creation, sstore, code deployment). Given that the state reservoir is only initialized if tx.gaslimit > 16m, so i expect the majority of state gas charging will take from the regular.

https://hackmd.io/@bFEBbZiVSAO0IURh9qzEFg/H1jEE_0xfl

a quick note, i know <@429719902363058177> has strong opinion for preserving the refunded gas in reservoir, probably it's still worthwhile to consider the alternative?

author: draganrakita
content:
It is not strong opinion but it is most logical one imo.

any gas (regular or reservoir) -> is spend on state gas -> on revert/refund state gas is put to reservoir.

What is proposed is maybe simple if you consider only one dimension:
regular gas -> is spend on state gas -> on revert/refund is returned to regular

but you have:
reservoir gas -> is spend on state gas -> on revert/refund is returned to reservoir
part of reservoir gas and part of regular gas -> is spend on state gas -> parts are returned to its own domain even if it was spend on state gas (this is confusing).

author: garyrong
content:
> part of reservoir gas and part of regular gas -> is spend on state gas -> parts are returned to its own domain even if it was spend on state gas (this is confusing).
well, i feel it's also logical. We borrow the gas bugdet from the regular and use it for state cost. If the cost is canceled, we return the borrowed part back to the original source

author: lu_pinto
content:
I think there's two separate discussions here.

> I guess we can generalize the dicussion to reservoir-based refund vs source-based refund. 
IMO they are different. The EIP-7702 worst case charges are essentially an overcharge, and what is given back afterwards is a correction, not a refund - it is part of the normal happy case, you don't need a halt or a revert for it to happen.
So I think since they are fundamentally different, they should be looked at separately.

author: lu_pinto
content:
Regarding the other case of reverts/halts during execution I also thought about completely getting rid of the reservoir as it would make implementations much simpler and the reservoir kind of annoys me a little from a design POV.
However, there's a catch. If you are giving back refunds from slot deletion during execution that would create some attack vectors where people would free state and get it back for computation as they are getting it back through the `gasLeft` pool.
These attacks are also exarcebated even more as you are transitioning into the hardfork, where people might rush into creating state right now and getting much more value out of it after the fork as the refund you get is much bigger.

Only if you hold on to refunds and give them back at the end (which is the behaviour right now) would it make sense to go that route. Maybe something worth considering. Why did we decide to change that behaviour again? Final refunds are capped at 20% today which is a good property.

So what I'm saying is, if you took X from `gasLeft` for state put it back into `gasLeft` in halt or revert. If you cleared a slot get the refund at the end of tx as today. No `reservoir` needed.

author: garyrong
content:
> However, there's a catch. If you are giving back refunds from slot deletion during execution that would create some attack vectors where people would free state and get it back for computation as they are getting it back through the gasLeft pool.
No, we don't refund the state gas if the state was pre-existing before the transaction. For the transient state clearing, we still charge the regular at least, i don't think we can attack it by playing around the slot creation-deletion

author: draganrakita
content: Dont have strong point here (on eip7702 refund), but this breaks a notion how other parts do the state refund.

author: draganrakita
content: On frame revert/halt, it is more obvious to me that is needs to go to reservoir. And impl vise this is simplest solution.
